title: Cybersecurity, Ethics and Privacy
summary: Information ethics, cybercrime, cybersecurity threats, privacy laws, codes of conduct, and ethical dilemmas in information systems.
content_html:
Cybersecurity, Ethics and Privacy

As digital systems become more pervasive, ethical conduct, privacy, and security become critical concerns for businesses and society.

Information Ethics

Ethics is the study and practice of principles governing the behaviour of people. Information ethics (or cyber ethics) focuses on moral issues arising from the development and use of information systems. Key focus areas include: privacy, accountability, consent, accuracy, authority, accessibility, intellectual property, and copyright.

Ethical Dilemmas

An ethical dilemma is a conflict between two moral imperatives. Examples in IS:

  • Publishing sensitive government data for transparency vs. committing a crime.
  • Making all data public vs. protecting personal privacy.
  • Scaled digital services vs. widening the digital divide.

Laws and Regulations

Laws: Rules that mandate or prohibit certain behaviour, carrying sanctions of a governing authority.

Ethics: Define socially acceptable behaviour -- may not carry legal sanctions.

In Australia, key legislation includes the Privacy Act 1988, the Australian Consumer Law, and the Criminal Code Act 1995 (cybercrime provisions).

Cybercrime and Cybersecurity

It is people, not computers, who create security threats: copying software, searching databases for sensitive information, creating viruses, hacking systems, and stealing or destroying information.

Common cybersecurity threats:

  • Malware: Viruses, ransomware, trojans, spyware.
  • Phishing: Deceptive emails/websites to steal credentials.
  • Social engineering: Manipulating people rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities.
  • Data breaches: Unauthorised access to confidential data.

Codes of Ethics

Professional bodies issue codes of ethics -- written guidelines to help members conduct themselves ethically. The Australian Computer Society (ACS) code emphasises: Primacy of Public Interest, Enhancement of Quality of Life, Honesty, Competence, Professional Development, and Professionalism.

Privacy and Data Protection

Examples of ethical IS privacy issues: banks using credit scoring to de-personalise customers, insurance companies accessing private medical data, digital music/movie piracy. These sit at the intersection of law, ethics, and business practice.

exercises.title: Information ethics focus
options: How to build faster computer systems
Moral issues arising from the development and use of information systems
exercises.answer: Moral issues arising from the development and use of information systems

exercises.title: Ethical dilemma
exercises.prompt: An ethical dilemma in IS is:
options: A technical bug causing data loss
A conflict between two moral imperatives
A legal violation by an employee
exercises.answer: A conflict between two moral imperatives

exercises.title: Cybersecurity source
exercises.prompt: According to the INF10024 perspective, who or what is ultimately responsible for cybersecurity threats?
options: People -- not computers -- who create and spread threats
exercises.answer: People -- not computers -- who create and spread threats

exercises.title: Phishing definition
options: Deceptive emails or websites designed to steal credentials or sensitive information
A method of encrypting database records
exercises.answer: Deceptive emails or websites designed to steal credentials or sensitive information

exercises.title: Law vs ethics
exercises.prompt: The key difference between laws and ethics is:
options: Laws are written by companies, ethics by governments
Laws carry sanctions of a governing authority; ethics define socially acceptable behaviour but may not carry legal sanctions
Ethics are always more strict than laws
Laws only apply to technology companies
exercises.answer: Laws carry sanctions of a governing authority; ethics define socially acceptable behaviour but may not carry legal sanctions

exercises.title: ACS code value
exercises.prompt: The Australian Computer Society code of ethics places which value FIRST?

exercises.title: Social engineering
options: People -- manipulating them rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities
exercises.answer: People -- manipulating them rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities

exercises.title: Privacy ethical issue
exercises.prompt: Which is an example of an information ethics privacy issue?
options: Insurance companies accessing private medical data to increase customer premiums
exercises.answer: Insurance companies accessing private medical data to increase customer premiums
